examples.title: Pseudocode: SLR Parser Construction Algorithm
examples.description: Pseudocode for building SLR parsing tables including closure, goto, and canonical collection
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Compute_Closure
INPUT: Set of items I, Grammar G
OUTPUT: Closure(I)

BEGIN
 Closure_I <- I
 repeat
 for each item [A -> α•Bβ] in Closure_I do
 if B is a non-terminal then
 for each production B -> γ in G do
 if [B -> •γ] not in Closure_I then
 add [B -> •γ] to Closure_I
 end if
 end for
 end if
 end for
 until no new items can be added
 return Closure_I
END

ALGORITHM Compute_Goto
INPUT: Set of items I, Grammar symbol X
OUTPUT: goto(I, X)

BEGIN
 J <- empty set
 for each item [A -> α•Xβ] in I do
 add [A -> αX•β] to J
 end for
 return Compute_Closure(J)
END

ALGORITHM Build_Canonical_Collection
INPUT: Augmented Grammar G'
OUTPUT: Collection C of LR(0) item sets

BEGIN
 C <- { Compute_Closure({[S' -> •S]}) }
 repeat
 for each item set I in C do
 for each grammar symbol X do
 J <- Compute_Goto(I, X)
 if J is not empty and J not in C then
 add J to C
 end if
 end for
 end for
 until no new item sets added
 return C
END

ALGORITHM Build_SLR_Table
INPUT: Grammar G (augmented), Collection C
OUTPUT: ACTION table, GOTO table

BEGIN
 for each state I_i in C do
 for each item [A -> α•aβ] in I_i do
 j <- index of Compute_Goto(I_i, a)
 if a is terminal then
 ACTION[i, a] <- "shift " + j
 end if
 end for
 
 for each item [A -> α•] in I_i do
 for each terminal a in FOLLOW(A) do
 ACTION[i, a] <- "reduce A -> α"
 end for
 end for
 
 if [S' -> S•] in I_i then
 ACTION[i, $] <- "accept"
 end if
 
 for each non-terminal A do
 j <- index of Compute_Goto(I_i, A)
 if j exists then
 GOTO[i, A] <- j
 end if
 end for
 end for
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the three core algorithms for SLR parser construction: (1) Closure computes all valid items by adding productions for non-terminals after the dot; (2) Goto computes the transition from one item set to another by moving the dot across a symbol; (3) Build_Canonical_Collection creates all LR(0) states by repeatedly applying goto until no new states emerge.

examples.title: Python: Complete SLR Parser Implementation
examples.description: Full Python implementation demonstrating LR(0) items, closure, goto, table construction, and parsing
examples.explanation: This Python implementation provides a complete SLR parser with: (1) Grammar analysis to extract terminals/non-terminals; (2) CLOSURE operation that adds items for non-terminals after the dot; (3) GOTO operation that moves the dot across symbols; (4) FOLLOW set computation for reduce actions; (5) Canonical collection construction for LR(0) states; (6) Full parsing table building with shift/reduce/accept actions. The parse() method demonstrates the actual parsing process with detailed trace output showing stack, input, and actions at each step.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n³) for table construction where n is grammar size; O(m) for parsing m tokens

examples.title: C: SLR Parsing Algorithm Implementation
examples.description: C implementation showing the SLR parsing algorithm and table-driven parsing
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates the SLR parsing algorithm with: (1) LR(0) item representation with dot position tracking; (2) CLOSURE computation using iterative expansion; (3) GOTO operation for state transitions; (4) Canonical collection building to create all LR(0) states; (5) FOLLOW set computation for reduce actions; (6) Full ACTION and GOTO table construction; (7) The actual parsing algorithm with shift, reduce, and accept operations. The parse_input() function shows detailed step-by-step parsing with stack state and actions at each step, making it easy to trace the bottom-up parsing process.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n²) for table construction; O(m) for parsing m tokens
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n²) for parsing tables where n is number of states
